questions.stem: This scanning electron micrograph shows the anterior end of a nematode with prominent cutting structures ringing its buccal cavity. What is the functional role of these structures?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anchoring to and cutting the intestinal mucosa to feed on blood

choices.label: C
choices.text: Boring through skeletal muscle to encyst

choices.label: E
choices.text: Attaching to red blood cells for locomotion

explanation.summary: Hookworms (Ancylostoma duodenale shown; Necator americanus uses cutting plates) use these buccal teeth/plates to grip and lacerate the small-bowel mucosa, feeding on blood and causing iron-deficiency anemia in heavy infections.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hookworms (Ancylostoma duodenale shown; Necator americanus uses cutting plates) use these buccal teeth/plates to grip and lacerate the small-bowel mucosa, feeding on blood and causing iron-deficiency anemia in heavy infections.

questions.stem: This cytology field shows elongated, bipyramidal (needle/spindle-shaped) purple crystals amid inflammatory cells from a patient with a parasitic infection. What are these crystals composed of?

explanation.summary: These are Charcot-Leyden crystals, formed from galectin-10 released by degranulating eosinophils; they signal eosinophil-rich processes such as parasitic or allergic disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: These are Charcot-Leyden crystals, formed from galectin-10 released by degranulating eosinophils; they signal eosinophil-rich processes such as parasitic or allergic disease.

questions.stem: This head CT of a patient with new-onset seizures shows numerous scattered calcified lesions throughout the brain parenchyma. Which condition best explains this appearance?

explanation.summary: Multiple calcified cysts scattered through the brain in a patient with seizures are characteristic of neurocysticercosis from T. solium larvae; it is a leading cause of adult-onset seizures in endemic regions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Multiple calcified cysts scattered through the brain in a patient with seizures are characteristic of neurocysticercosis from T. solium larvae; it is a leading cause of adult-onset seizures in endemic regions.

questions.stem: This radiograph of the lower limbs shows numerous small, rice-grain-shaped calcified densities scattered within the soft tissues and muscles. What do these represent?

explanation.summary: Disseminated cysticercosis produces multiple calcified larval cysts in skeletal muscle, seen as scattered oval/rice-shaped calcifications on plain films, and can occur alongside neurocysticercosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Disseminated cysticercosis produces multiple calcified larval cysts in skeletal muscle, seen as scattered oval/rice-shaped calcifications on plain films, and can occur alongside neurocysticercosis.

questions.stem: Stool microscopy reveals a round egg with a thick, radially striated shell; an arrow points to small hooklets inside. These eggs are diagnostic of which parasite group?

explanation.summary: Taenia eggs are round with a thick radially striated shell and contain a six-hooked oncosphere; the eggs of the different Taenia species are morphologically identical and cannot distinguish between them.
explanation.whyCorrect: Taenia eggs are round with a thick radially striated shell and contain a six-hooked oncosphere; the eggs of the different Taenia species are morphologically identical and cannot distinguish between them.

questions.stem: This stained whole-mount shows the scolex of an intestinal tapeworm bearing both suckers and a crown of hooks (an armed rostellum). Which species is most consistent with this finding?

explanation.summary: T. solium has an armed scolex with a rostellum bearing two rows of hooks, whereas T. saginata has four suckers but no rostellum or hooks.
explanation.whyCorrect: T. solium has an armed scolex with a rostellum bearing two rows of hooks, whereas T. saginata has four suckers but no rostellum or hooks.

questions.stem: This stained gravid proglottid is used to speciate a tapeworm by counting the lateral uterine branches. Seeing fewer than about 13 branches per side indicates which species?

explanation.summary: T. solium gravid proglottids have 7-13 lateral uterine branches per side, while T. saginata has 12-30; counting branches distinguishes the two species.
explanation.whyCorrect: T. solium gravid proglottids have 7-13 lateral uterine branches per side, while T. saginata has 12-30; counting branches distinguishes the two species.

questions.stem: This proglottid shows a central uterus arranged in a rosette (flower-petal) pattern. This feature distinguishes which tapeworm from Taenia species?

explanation.summary: Diphyllobothrium proglottids have a central, rosette-shaped uterus, which distinguishes them from the laterally branching uterus of Taenia proglottids.
explanation.whyCorrect: Diphyllobothrium proglottids have a central, rosette-shaped uterus, which distinguishes them from the laterally branching uterus of Taenia proglottids.

questions.stem: This schematic illustrates a scope passed down the trachea into the lungs while saline is instilled and aspirated back. What is the primary diagnostic purpose of this procedure in suspected disseminated helminth infection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recover larvae/organisms from the lower airways for diagnosis

explanation.summary: Bronchoalveolar lavage instills and aspirates sterile saline to sample the alveolar space; in hyperinfection it can recover migrating Strongyloides larvae when sputum is unrevealing.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bronchoalveolar lavage instills and aspirates sterile saline to sample the alveolar space; in hyperinfection it can recover migrating Strongyloides larvae when sputum is unrevealing.

questions.stem: This stool wet mount shows an oval egg with a thin, smooth shell and a clear space between the developing embryo and the shell. This morphology is characteristic of which infection?

explanation.summary: Hookworm eggs are thin-shelled ovals with a segmented embryo and a characteristic clear gap beneath the smooth shell; Ancylostoma and Necator eggs are morphologically indistinguishable.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hookworm eggs are thin-shelled ovals with a segmented embryo and a characteristic clear gap beneath the smooth shell; Ancylostoma and Necator eggs are morphologically indistinguishable.

questions.stem: This stool micrograph shows a rounded egg with a thick shell covered by an irregular, knobby outer coat. Which organism and egg type does this represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fertilized Ascaris lumbricoides egg with mammillated coat

explanation.summary: Fertilized Ascaris eggs are round with a thick shell and a bumpy (mammillated) albuminous coat; recognizing this coat distinguishes them from the smooth-shelled eggs of hookworm and Enterobius.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fertilized Ascaris eggs are round with a thick shell and a bumpy (mammillated) albuminous coat; recognizing this coat distinguishes them from the smooth-shelled eggs of hookworm and Enterobius.

questions.stem: This specimen shows a tiny, white, thread-like female worm with a pin-shaped pointed tail. A child with nocturnal perianal itching is most likely infected with which organism?

explanation.summary: Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) is a small white nematode whose gravid females migrate to the anus at night to lay eggs, producing the classic perianal pruritus in children.
explanation.whyCorrect: Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) is a small white nematode whose gravid females migrate to the anus at night to lay eggs, producing the classic perianal pruritus in children.

questions.stem: This micrograph shows transparent, elongated eggs that are flattened along one side. This asymmetric 'D-shaped' morphology is characteristic of which parasite?

explanation.summary: Pinworm eggs are colorless and ovoid with one flattened side (D-shaped); they are best recovered with cellulose (Scotch) tape from the perianal region in the morning.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pinworm eggs are colorless and ovoid with one flattened side (D-shaped); they are best recovered with cellulose (Scotch) tape from the perianal region in the morning.

questions.stem: A stool slide shows a rounded egg with a thick shell whose outer surface is irregular and knobby. Which parasitic infection does the patient most likely have?

explanation.summary: The round egg with a nodular (mammillated) coat is a fertilized Ascaris lumbricoides egg; hookworms have smooth thin-shelled eggs and Strongyloides is usually identified by larvae rather than eggs in stool.
explanation.whyCorrect: The round egg with a nodular (mammillated) coat is a fertilized Ascaris lumbricoides egg; hookworms have smooth thin-shelled eggs and Strongyloides is usually identified by larvae rather than eggs in stool.

questions.stem: This schematic highlights a dangerous cycle unique to T. solium in which the host becomes reinfected by their own eggs. What is this process called?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Zoonotic spillover from a reservoir host

explanation.summary: When a person ingests T. solium eggs from their own feces, the eggs hatch and larvae disseminate to tissues, causing cysticercosis; this self-reinfection is termed autoinfection.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a person ingests T. solium eggs from their own feces, the eggs hatch and larvae disseminate to tissues, causing cysticercosis; this self-reinfection is termed autoinfection.

questions.stem: This flow schematic traces larvae penetrating skin, finding blood vessels/lymphatics, migrating to the lungs, then being coughed up and swallowed to mature. Which helminth's infectious cycle is summarized?

explanation.summary: The skin-penetration to lung-migration to gut-maturation sequence is the classic infectious cycle of Strongyloides stercoralis (shared in broad strokes with hookworms), distinguishing it from fecal-orally acquired worms.
explanation.whyCorrect: The skin-penetration to lung-migration to gut-maturation sequence is the classic infectious cycle of Strongyloides stercoralis (shared in broad strokes with hookworms), distinguishing it from fecal-orally acquired worms.

questions.stem: This CDC life-cycle diagram contrasts a free-living cycle with a parasitic cycle and highlights autoinfection returning larvae to the small intestine. Which unique feature best explains lifelong infection by this organism?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Autoinfection allowing indefinite reinfection of the same host

explanation.summary: Strongyloides stercoralis is unusual among helminths in performing autoinfection: rhabditiform larvae mature and re-penetrate the gut mucosa or perianal skin, sustaining infection for years and driving hyperinfection under immunosuppression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Strongyloides stercoralis is unusual among helminths in performing autoinfection: rhabditiform larvae mature and re-penetrate the gut mucosa or perianal skin, sustaining infection for years and driving hyperinfection under immunosuppression.

questions.stem: This CDC diagram shows filariform larvae penetrating the skin of a foot, migrating to the lungs, being swallowed, and maturing to adults in the small intestine that release eggs in feces. It best depicts the cycle of which parasites?

explanation.summary: Hookworm larvae penetrate intact skin (often the foot), migrate through the lungs, and mature in the gut; unlike Strongyloides they cannot autoinfect, so eggs must pass in stool and develop in soil.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hookworm larvae penetrate intact skin (often the foot), migrate through the lungs, and mature in the gut; unlike Strongyloides they cannot autoinfect, so eggs must pass in stool and develop in soil.

questions.stem: This CDC life-cycle diagram shows a person ingesting embryonated eggs, larvae hatching and migrating through the lungs, then maturing into large adults in the small intestine. Which organism is depicted?

explanation.summary: Ascaris (the giant roundworm) is acquired fecal-orally by ingesting embryonated eggs; larvae undergo lung migration before maturing into large adults in the gut, and unlike hookworms/Strongyloides it does not penetrate skin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ascaris (the giant roundworm) is acquired fecal-orally by ingesting embryonated eggs; larvae undergo lung migration before maturing into large adults in the gut, and unlike hookworms/Strongyloides it does not penetrate skin.

questions.stem: This CDC diagram shows embryonated eggs ingested fecal-orally, larvae hatching in the small intestine, and gravid females migrating to the perianal region at night to deposit eggs. Which organism is shown?

explanation.summary: Pinworm has a simple fecal-oral cycle with no tissue migration: gravid females crawl to the perianal skin at night to lay eggs, driving itching, scratching, and self-reinfection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pinworm has a simple fecal-oral cycle with no tissue migration: gravid females crawl to the perianal skin at night to lay eggs, driving itching, scratching, and self-reinfection.
